subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Bachelor’s Degree from an accredited university
Majored in Business Administration with a focus on nonprofit management
Completed elective coursework in marketing, finance, and communications
Participated in workshops on fundraising and investor relations during university
Attended online certificate programs in startup fundraising post-graduation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Current job title: Senior Fundraising Manager
Oversees fundraising strategy and execution for a startup organization
Responsible for investor outreach, pitch deck development, and market research
Acts as lead negotiator during funding rounds
Manages relationships with existing and prospective investors

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the technology startup sector
Focuses primarily on early-stage venture fundraising
Has experience with both impact-driven and revenue-driven investment models
Industry intersects with fintech and SaaS verticals
Engages with angel investors, venture capitalists, and family offices

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Works at a startup with fewer than 50 employees
Company culture emphasizes agility, innovation, and cross-functional collaboration
Flat organizational structure with direct access to executive leadership
Remote/hybrid work environment with distributed teams
Regularly interacts with founders, product, and legal teams

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Business operates in the B2B SaaS market
Targets clients in the enterprise and SME segments
Focuses on scalable solutions addressing industry-specific needs
Operates at the intersection of tech innovation and social impact
Actively seeks both financial and strategic investors

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 6-10 years of professional fundraising experience
Has been in the current Senior Fundraising Manager role for 3 years
Previously held similar roles at two other startups
Cumulative fundraising experience spans both seed and Series A-B rounds

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Holds a senior individual contributor position
Leads fundraising functions with significant autonomy
Acts as primary point of contact for investor relations
Participates in company strategy and planning meetings
Mentors junior fundraising staff and interns

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Conducts detailed market research to identify and rank potential investors
Prepares and customizes pitch decks based on investor profiles
Leads outreach campaigns, automates follow-ups, and manages investor communications
Performs due diligence checks and reviews legal documents for compliance
Negotiates terms and acts as the main liaison during funding negotiations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends approximately 30% of time on market/investor research
Devotes 20% of work week to document and pitch deck preparation
Allocates 25% to investor outreach and follow-ups
15% of time is spent on negotiation and legal review
10% focuses on relationship building and stakeholder updates

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Exercises high autonomy in setting outreach strategy and investor targeting
Has authority to negotiate initial terms and bring recommendations to leadership
Independently prepares market research and analysis reports
Consults with founders for final investment decisions
Empowered to select and implement new tools or workflows

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Customizing pitch decks to align with different investor types
Building and maintaining relationships with existing investors
Drafting outreach messages and follow-up email templates
Synthesizing market research findings into actionable insights
Managing stakeholder communications and monthly updates

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Negotiating term sheets and identifying hidden unfavorable clauses
Comparing multiple investor offers against market standards
Conducting compliance checks for regulatory and legal requirements
Coordinating cross-platform investor communications (email, LinkedIn, Whatsapp)
Automating outreach to portfolio companies with personalized messaging

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Scraping and updating investor databases regularly
Ranking and scoring investors based on alignment and reference checks
Generating and sending cold outreach emails in bulk
Following up on unanswered investor communications
Populating data rooms with standardized legal and financial documents

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Finds storytelling and pitch deck creation highly engaging
Enjoys strategic negotiation and relationship building
Considers database scraping and manual data entry tedious
Finds repeated email follow-ups monotonous
Prefers tasks involving creativity and strategic thinking

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Manual data scraping from multiple sources is time-consuming
Difficulty in integrating data across different CRM and outreach tools
Repetitive customization of investor communications across platforms
High volume of administrative tasks reduces time for relationship building
Inconsistent data formats when consolidating investor feedback

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Productivity peaks when working on tasks that require creativity and strategy
High skill level in relationship management makes these tasks enjoyable
Low enjoyment and productivity during repetitive administrative work
Skill development in negotiation has improved job satisfaction
Learning new automation tools increases both enjoyment and efficiency

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Proactive and enthusiastic about adopting new tech solutions
Views technology as a key enabler for efficiency in fundraising
Regularly experiments with new tools to streamline workflows
Seeks out webinars and online demos for emerging fundraising platforms

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to new software with minimal formal training
Comfortable exploring new features and troubleshooting issues independently
Frequently helps colleagues onboard to new fundraising tools
Has a track record of piloting new CRM and outreach platforms

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to invest several hours monthly in learning advanced tool features
Attends industry workshops and vendor training sessions when available
Balances training time with workload by prioritizing tools with clear ROI
Sees tech training as essential to maintaining a competitive edge

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the promise of reducing manual workload
Main barrier is the occasional lack of time due to tight fundraising deadlines
Prioritizes learning tools that integrate well with existing systems
Less inclined to adopt tools that require complex onboarding or disrupt workflow

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers often share recommendations for new fundraising platforms
Management encourages adoption of technologies that improve efficiency
Participates in peer-led tool demos and best practice sharing sessions
Positive team culture around tech experimentation and feedback

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses HubSpot CRM to manage investor pipeline and communications
Relies on Excel and Google Sheets for financial modeling and data aggregation
Employs Pitch.com and Canva for pitch deck creation
Utilizes LinkedIn Sales Navigator for investor research and outreach
Leverages Zapier for workflow automation between tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Gmail and Outlook for daily investor correspondence
DocuSign for digital signatures on NDAs and contracts
Slack for internal team communication
Dropbox and Google Drive for storing and sharing due diligence documents
Calendly for scheduling investor meetings

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Proficient in using DocSend to share and track pitch deck engagement
Experienced with Airtable for building custom investor databases
Familiar with Crunchbase and PitchBook for investor and market research
Understands the basics of Salesforce CRM, though not used daily

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasionally faces syncing issues between CRM and email platforms
Manual data transfer required from LinkedIn to CRM
Limited automation between document storage and investor tracking tools
Integration gaps sometimes lead to duplicated data entry
API limitations hinder seamless updating of investor databases

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands core AI/ML concepts such as natural language processing and predictive analytics
Familiar with prompt engineering to improve LLM output
Can explain basic differences between supervised and unsupervised learning
Has read industry articles on AI in fundraising and deal sourcing
Comfortable using terms like 'training data', 'fine-tuning', and 'model inference'

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses OpenAI’s ChatGPT weekly for pitch deck drafting and email generation
Employs Clay for automating investor research and generating outreach lists
Leverages Lavender AI to optimize cold email subject lines and content
Tests Copy.ai for drafting personalized investor follow-ups
Experiments with Google Bard for market research summaries

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Applies LLMs weekly to generate, edit, or tailor pitch materials
Uses AI tools several times a week for scraping and summarizing investor data
Relies on AI for automating repetitive messaging and cold outreach tasks
Employs AI-powered platforms to analyze competitor fundraising strategies
Checks AI-generated legal summaries against original documents for compliance

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI-generated investor messaging improved email response rates by 20%
LLM occasionally produced generic content requiring significant human editing
AI flagged an unfavorable term in a term sheet that was missed during manual review
An automated outreach campaign included outdated company data, highlighting the need for regular database updates
AI-powered sentiment analysis misinterpreted investor feedback tone, necessitating manual review

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Organization provides access to online AI tool tutorials and documentation
Peers often share AI prompt templates and workflow tips in Slack channels
No formal in-house AI training program established yet
Occasional lunch-and-learn sessions cover new AI features or best practices
Relies on external webinars and online communities for advanced AI learning

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI for complex tasks like negotiation
Uses independent mode for routine content generation and data scraping
Appreciates the option to provide feedback and refine AI outputs iteratively
Finds collaborative AI workflows helpful for brainstorming and ideation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as an intelligent assistant for drafting and research tasks
Uses AI as a co-worker during data-heavy workflow automation
Relies on AI as an advisor for benchmarking term sheets and deal terms
Maintains final decision-making authority on all critical outputs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Highly open to integrating AI-driven workflows that save time
Actively experiments with new AI features as they become available
Encourages colleagues to adopt AI tools for repetitive or data-intensive tasks
Provides feedback to AI vendors to improve workflow integration

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces for brainstorming and content generation
Uses command-based prompts for structured data tasks and automation
Finds conversational UIs more intuitive for exploring new ideas
Appreciates hybrid interfaces offering both quick commands and natural language interaction

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Investor negotiation and relationship management require deep domain expertise
Tailoring pitches depends on nuanced understanding of investor psychology
Due diligence checks benefit from industry-specific regulatory knowledge
AI assists with data aggregation but human judgment remains critical for final decisions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for generating drafts and initial analyses but always reviews critical outputs
Cross-checks all AI-generated legal and financial documents before sharing externally
Views AI as a valuable second set of eyes, not a replacement for domain expertise
Trust level increases with transparent, explainable outputs from AI tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers high automation for repetitive data scraping and analysis
Maintains human oversight for investor communications and negotiation
Combines AI-generated drafts with manual customization for pitch decks
Delegates routine follow-ups to AI, but handles sensitive investor queries personally

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for tasks with low risk and clear guidelines
Finds full automation threatening if it risks losing personal touch in investor relations
Requires human review for any AI-driven legal or compliance processes
Willing to automate more when AI tools demonstrate consistent accuracy and transparency

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: AI has enhanced the importance of strategic thinking and relationship management
Routine technical skills like data entry are becoming less critical
Storytelling and negotiation remain irreplaceable by AI
AI frees up time to focus on high-value, human-centric tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Prompt engineering and AI workflow design are now part of daily work
Responsible for evaluating the reliability and biases in AI-generated outputs
Required to learn integration of AI tools with existing platforms
Acts as a resource for team members on best practices for AI adoption

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Minimal concern about AI replacing core fundraising and negotiation responsibilities
Expects some administrative and data processing tasks to be fully automated
Sees AI as a tool to augment, not replace, the human element of the role
Monitors industry trends to stay ahead of potential AI-driven disruptions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: All AI-generated communications are reviewed before sending to investors
Manual approval required for final legal and compliance documents
Can pause or edit automated outreach sequences at any time
Maintains logs of AI activity for post-process review and audit

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Company is increasingly supportive of AI pilots and experimentation
Recent policies encourage responsible AI use with mandatory human oversight
Team meetings now regularly include updates on AI tool performance
Management seeks employee input before adopting new AI-driven workflows

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will democratize access to fundraising intelligence for startups
Expects AI to accelerate deal flow and market transparency
Anticipates new ethical and regulatory challenges as AI adoption grows
Sees AI as a catalyst for innovation in the fundraising sector

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Supports transparent and explainable AI systems for workplace use
Concerned about potential bias in AI-generated investor scoring and profiling
Believes in maintaining human accountability for critical fundraising decisions
Advocates for clear disclosure when AI is used in investor communications

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Desires an AI-driven investor matching engine that factors in nuanced company metrics
Wants seamless AI integration across all communication and document platforms
Seeks advanced AI support for regulatory compliance and cross-border fundraising
Would benefit from AI that can simulate negotiation scenarios and outcomes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects core fundraising tasks to become more strategic as AI handles routine work
Foresees increased focus on relationship management and creative deal structuring
Predicts greater reliance on data-driven insights for investor targeting
Anticipates needing to upskill in AI oversight and ethical data use

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Implement regular training on new AI tools and best practices
Establish clear guidelines for human oversight of AI-generated outputs
Invest in customizable AI platforms that integrate with existing workflows
Foster a culture of experimentation while maintaining ethical standards
